title: Jupyter Pod
text:
Take note of the token at the end of the output. We will use it to login Jupyter Notebook. Do not close this terminal window or type ```Ctrl - C``` to terminate the process. 1. **Setup port forwarding**
In a separate terminal window on your local machine run port-forward to access the pod. Here we tell kubectl to forward any request to your local host port 8888 from a remote host (container) port 8888. kubectl port-forward gpu-pod-example 8888:8888 A successful command results in the output Forwarding from 127.0.0.1:8888 -> 8888 Forwarding from [::1]:8888 -> 8888 1. **Connect to Jupyter**
In a new web browser window connect to an address **localhost:8888**, you should see the following page: Enter the previously saved token on the **Password or Token** line at the top of the page. Optionally, to set a password follow direcitons on the page. Click appropriate **Log in** button. You get a Jupyter notebook with access to tensorflow: **Note:** Once you are finished, we'll [tear down](#step4) our setup. ### Step 4: Tear down It is important to tear down everything once we are done with the Jupyter to free up resources for others. 1. **Close your web browser window** with Jupyter notebook once you are finished. 1. **Shutdown the proxy**
In the terminal window where you executed port forwarding command, press `Ctrl-C`. This will stop local machine connection to the remote container. 1. **Shutdown the Jupyter Server**
Press `Ctrl-C` twice on the terminal window where you are logged in on a container and where jupyter server is running. You should see something along:
